feedback.text: Ladies and gentlemen, video games are a time-distributor of many people, and more than one third of the world’s population plays video games. The video games industry is therefore a strong market, but it is also vulnerable to consumer-friendly practices. One such practice is that videogame developers and publishers to schedule reserved games that have been bought, make them unplayable without any warning. The consumer is then presented alone and a good which he had been bought was denied to him even though he was lawfully owned by him. It is not a question of requiring every game to be playable forever, with servers provided by the publishers, but of something simple, such as a single player mode or a public possibility of continuing to support the game itself, so that the legitimately purchased game remains in the consumer’s hands. This obsolescence is not only a by-product, as in the case of electrical equipment that breaks down over time, but it is planned obsolencing, which leads the customer to buy the publisher’s new product. It is harmful to customers and also makes the Conservation video game almost impossible. This is a problem for many EU citizens, which is why EU citizens’ initiatives such as ‘Stop Killing Games’ have 1.438.553 signatures (position: 29/07/2025 18:18), who hopes that this issue of deliberate destruction of video games will also be addressed at a political level. Accordingly, this is also a call for this problematic practice to be included and addressed in the context of the protection of consumers. Thank you very much for your attention.
